architecture_recommendations: Keep SQLite/report chain as the source of truth until a durable adapter can replay all service-request lifecycle states without changing semantics.

Build the next local artifact as a Temporal/Inngest adapter manifest over existing service-request rows: events, workflow IDs, activities/steps, pause points, and side-effect gates; no installs, no workers, no external services.
Treat OpenAI Agents SDK as the specialist runtime only after the model/API gate has provider/model/cost/data/output scope and a registered pool; it should not replace the CEO ledger.
Use Prefect and Dagster as operational references for local scan concurrency, pool capacity, sensors, skip reasons, run keys, and cursors.

Adopt 12-factor agent principles into schema wording: execution state equals business state, humans are contacted through explicit packets/forms, agents stay small/focused, and every launch/pause/resume has a simple local API.

scorecard.next_local_proof: Add adapter manifests that emit Temporal/Inngest-compatible events from existing DB rows without running external engines.
scorecard.why: Already contains lanes, tasks, artifacts, outcomes, traces, service requests, decision packets, forms, and chain validation with no external dependencies.

scorecard.risk: Requires service/database/server adoption and deterministic workflow discipline.
scorecard.why: Best durability semantics for approval-bound work that may pause/resume for days or months; replay/event-history maps cleanly to our traces.

scorecard.risk: Cloud/service posture must be reviewed before any deployment or account use.
scorecard.role: event-driven durable function and trigger layer
scorecard.why: Good fit for event-triggered seeker/manager scans, retryable steps, throttling, and cron/event workflows.

scorecard.risk: Cost/provider/model/data-scope gates remain mandatory before model/API execution.
scorecard.why: Native primitives match specialists, handoffs, guardrails, human review, tracing, sandboxing, and evals.

scorecard.role: local ops runner, pool/concurrency pattern reference
scorecard.why: Nice Python flow/task ergonomics and pool controls for local scans/report fan-out, but less purpose-built for approval-bound agent lifecycles.
Sensors, run keys, cursors, asset checks are excellent for deduplicated evidence freshness.
Strong alignment with explicit business state, pause/resume, human contact as tool calls, small agents, and owned control flow.

sources.architecture_use: Use as the model-backed specialist/handoff/guardrail runtime after local approval and cost gates, not as the authoritative ledger.
observed_points: OpenAI positions the Agents SDK around code-first agent apps, specialist definitions, runtime loop/state, sandbox agents, orchestration/handoffs, guardrails and human review, results/state, tools/MCP, tracing, and evals.
This maps strongly to our seeker/manager/service-worker runtime once model/API execution is explicitly approved.
sources.title: OpenAI Agents SDK - choose your starting point

sources.architecture_use: Strong candidate for future durable service-request lifecycle engine once the SQLite packet schemas stabilize.
observed_points: Temporal is described as a scalable runtime for durable function executions, with Workflow Executions that maintain state through failures using Event History.
Temporal applications can consist of very large numbers of lightweight Workflow Executions; suspended workflows consume little/no compute, and workflows are resumable, recoverable, and reactive.
sources.title: Temporal Platform docs - durable execution and workflows

sources.architecture_use: Important design pattern: keep CEO/manager state deterministic; isolate browser/API/model/payment work as gated Activities.
observed_points: Temporal records workflow commands/events in Event History, replays history to restore state, and requires deterministic workflow decisions.
External interactions such as API calls, database queries, LLM invocations, and file I/O belong in Activities, whose results are recorded and reused on replay.
sources.title: Temporal Workflow docs - replay and Activities

sources.architecture_use: Useful as a local operations runner candidate; not the first authority for service-request approval state.
observed_points: Prefect workflows are Python functions decorated as flows; tasks can run concurrently with submit/map and futures.
This is a good fit for local scans, queue rebuilds, report generation, and artifact fan-out where Python ergonomics matter more than years-long durable state.
sources.title: Prefect docs - flows, tasks, concurrency

sources.architecture_use: Useful pattern for our service-worker pool registry: pool pause/resume, preview, capacity, and restricted templates.
observed_points: Prefect work pools can be created, inspected, previewed, paused/resumed, deleted, and assigned concurrency limits via CLI/UI/API.
Base job templates can restrict deployment infrastructure customization in secure environments.
sources.title: Prefect docs - work pools

sources.architecture_use: Good fit for evidence/source freshness sensors and deduplicated scan triggers; less direct fit for approval-bound service-worker lifecycles.
observed_points: Dagster sensors respond to internal/external events, either launching runs or yielding skip reasons.
Run keys prevent duplicate runs; cursors support high-volume event tracking.
sources.title: Dagster docs - sensors

sources.architecture_use: Strong lightweight candidate for event-driven manager/seeker triggers and workflow steps after local schemas are stable.
observed_points: Inngest describes itself as an event-driven durable execution platform for reliable code on any platform, handling backend infrastructure, queueing, scaling, concurrency, throttling, rate limiting, and observability.
It exposes agent resources, a dev-server MCP, and LLM-friendly docs.
sources.title: Inngest docs - durable event-driven functions

sources.architecture_use: Useful for future event-to-work-packet pipeline and retryable local proof steps.
observed_points: Inngest functions are triggered by events, cron schedules, or webhooks; flow control includes concurrency/throttling; steps create retriable checkpoints.
Patterns include background jobs, delayed functions, cron functions, and workflows.
sources.title: Inngest docs - functions and steps

sources.architecture_use: Matches our current SQLite-first approach: keep business state explicit, use small focused workers, and model human approvals as first-class tool/workflow events.
observed_points: The guide argues production agents are mostly software with LLM steps inserted carefully, and recommends principles such as owning prompts/context/control flow, unifying execution and business state, launch/pause/resume APIs, contacting humans with tool calls, small focused agents, and stateless reducers.
It cautions against overcommitting to frameworks before understanding the control flow and quality bar.
sources.title: HumanLayer 12-factor agents

sources.architecture_use: Reference for IDE-side human experience and parallel worktree/session UX, not core backend authority.
observed_points: The current HumanLayer repo has shifted toward CodeLayer, an open-source IDE for orchestrating AI coding agents, with workflows for complex codebases and parallel Claude Code sessions.
It is useful as a UX/workflow reference for multi-agent coding, but not a direct service-worker control plane for money-path operations.
sources.title: HumanLayer / CodeLayer repository

title: Wave 9 durable orchestration and human-in-the-loop control-plane research
